subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Attention, Arousal, and Cognition (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study examined whether background noise type affects reading comprehension and self-reported stress in college students.
Participants
One hundred and eighty undergraduate students enrolled in an introductory psychology course at a large urban university volunteered to participate for extra credit. After removing data from 12 participants who reported hearing impairments or did not complete the full protocol, the final sample consisted of 168 participants (56% women, 44% men). Ages ranged from 17 to 26 years (mean age = 20.2, standard deviation = 1.9). The racial/ethnic composition of the final sample was: 38% White/European American, 29% Black/African American, 19% Hispanic/Latino, 9% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other or Unreported.
Method
Upon arriving at the laboratory, participants were randomly assigned to one of three noise conditions using a random number table. All participants sat in individual soundproofed study carrels fitted with headphones and were asked to read a 1,200-word expository passage about climate policy for 20 minutes. During reading, participants heard one of the following audio tracks through their headphones:
• Quiet condition: ambient silence (background hum of HVAC at approximately 30 dB)
• Nature sounds condition: a looping recording of a forest stream and birdsong (approximately 55 dB)
• Cafeteria noise condition: a looping recording of a busy cafeteria with overlapping conversations and background music (approximately 72 dB)
Immediately after reading, participants completed a 20-item multiple-choice reading comprehension test based on the passage. Each correct item earned one point for a maximum score of 20. Participants also completed the 10-item Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-10), a validated self-report measure of current stress, with higher scores indicating greater perceived stress (range 0–40).
Results and Discussion
The researchers hypothesized that nature sounds would support comprehension and reduce stress compared to both quiet and cafeteria noise conditions. The results are summarized in the table below.

Mean Scores by Noise Condition

| Measure | Quiet Condition | Nature Sounds Condition | Cafeteria Noise Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reading comprehension score (out of 20) | 14.2 | 15.6 | 11.3 |
| Perceived stress score (out of 40) | 16.8 | 13.4 | 22.7 |

Statistical analyses confirmed that differences among the three conditions were statistically significant for both measures. The nature sounds condition produced the highest comprehension scores and the lowest stress scores. The cafeteria noise condition produced the lowest comprehension scores and the highest stress scores. The researchers concluded that semantically meaningful noise (overlapping speech) is particularly disruptive to reading comprehension, while natural ambient sound may provide a mild arousal benefit.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies the research method as an experiment, referencing random assignment or manipulation of the independent variable.

required_elements: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to one of three noise conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to one of three noise conditions.

required_elements: Because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (noise type) and randomly assigned participants to conditions, this is an experiment.
alternatives.correct_example: Because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (noise type) and randomly assigned participants to conditions, this is an experiment.

wrong_examples: The researchers used a survey. [Incorrect method identification.]
This was a correlational study. [Incorrect — random assignment and IV manipulation make it an experiment.]
common_traps: Confusing an experiment with a correlational study
Identifying the reading comprehension test as the research method

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of reading comprehension as used in the study.

scoring_points.description: States a measurable or quantifiable definition of reading comprehension as operationalized in the study — specifically the score on the 20-item multiple-choice test based on the climate policy passage.

required_elements: Reading comprehension was measured as the number of correct answers on a 20-item multiple-choice reading comprehension test.
alternatives.correct_example: Reading comprehension was measured as the number of correct answers on a 20-item multiple-choice reading comprehension test.

required_elements: The operational definition of reading comprehension is the score on the 20-question multiple-choice test administered immediately after reading.
alternatives.correct_example: The operational definition of reading comprehension is the score on the 20-question multiple-choice test administered immediately after reading.

wrong_examples: Reading comprehension means how well someone understands what they read. [Conceptual definition, not the operationalization used in the study.]
Reading comprehension was measured by the stress scale. [Incorrect — the PSS-10 measured stress, not reading comprehension.]
common_traps: Providing a conceptual rather than operational definition
Confusing the reading comprehension measure with the stress measure

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the relationship between noise condition and perceived stress.

scoring_points.description: Accurately describes what the means indicate — that cafeteria noise was associated with the highest stress (22.7), nature sounds with the lowest (13.4), and quiet in between (16.8) — AND explains what this pattern indicates in the context of the study.

required_elements: Greater perceived stress was associated with the cafeteria noise condition, and nature sounds were associated with the least stress — indicating that the type of background noise meaningfully affected participants' stress levels.
alternatives.correct_example: Greater perceived stress was associated with the cafeteria noise condition, and nature sounds were associated with the least stress — indicating that the type of background noise meaningfully affected participants' stress levels.

required_elements: As noise became more semantically meaningful (from nature sounds to cafeteria noise), self-reported stress increased, with cafeteria noise producing the highest mean stress score of 22.7.
alternatives.correct_example: As noise became more semantically meaningful (from nature sounds to cafeteria noise), self-reported stress increased, with cafeteria noise producing the highest mean stress score of 22.7.

wrong_examples: The cafeteria noise group had a stress score of 22.7. [States only the value without indicating what this means in context.]
common_traps: Reporting individual mean values without explaining what the pattern indicates
Confusing the comprehension scores with the stress scores

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers applied in conducting this study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies at least one ethical guideline actually applied in the study, grounded in evidence from the study description.

required_elements: Participants gave informed consent before the study began.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants gave informed consent before the study began.

required_elements: Participation was voluntary — students volunteered for extra credit and were not required to participate.
alternatives.correct_example: Participation was voluntary — students volunteered for extra credit and were not required to participate.

required_elements: Participants with hearing impairments were excluded to protect them from undue burden.
alternatives.correct_example: Participants with hearing impairments were excluded to protect them from undue burden.

wrong_examples: The researchers should have debriefed participants. [Identifies what should have happened, not what was done.]
common_traps: Identifying an ethical guideline not supported by the study description
Describing an ethical concern rather than a guideline that was followed

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: If students assert the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must support both a way it is generalizable and a way it is not to earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Explains the extent to which the study is generalizable with a specific claim about a population AND specific supporting evidence referencing participant variables from the study.

required_elements: The findings may not be generalizable beyond college students because all participants were undergraduates from a single urban university.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may not be generalizable beyond college students because all participants were undergraduates from a single urban university.

required_elements: The findings may be somewhat generalizable to young adults because the sample was demographically diverse across racial/ethnic groups.
alternatives.correct_example: The findings may be somewhat generalizable to young adults because the sample was demographically diverse across racial/ethnic groups.

required_elements: The results may not apply to older adults or children because participants ranged only from age 17 to 26.
alternatives.correct_example: The results may not apply to older adults or children because participants ranged only from age 17 to 26.

wrong_examples: The study is generalizable. [No evidence provided.]
The study had a large sample. [No specific claim about to whom findings generalize.]
common_traps: Asserting generalizability without citing specific participant characteristics
Claiming partial generalizability without addressing both directions

parts.prompt: Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the hypothesis that nature sounds would support comprehension and reduce stress compared to both quiet and cafeteria noise conditions.
parts.scoring_notes: Partial credit (1 pt) is awarded for using evidence from the study without fully explaining the connection to the hypothesis, or for explaining without using a specific finding. Full credit (2 pts) requires a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND an explanation of how it supports or refutes the hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: 1 point (partial): Uses specific evidence from the study OR accurately explains the relationship between findings and the hypothesis, but not both together in a fully connected argument.

required_elements: Uses a result from the table (e.g., comprehension or stress scores) in relation to the hypothesis without a complete explanation.
alternatives.correct_example: The nature sounds condition had the highest comprehension score (15.6), which supports the hypothesis.

wrong_examples: The hypothesis was supported. [No evidence cited and no explanation.]
common_traps: Citing a result without explaining how it supports or refutes the hypothesis
Explaining a relationship without referencing specific data

scoring_points.description: 1 point (full): Uses a specific, accurately interpreted finding AND explains how that finding supports or refutes the hypothesis about nature sounds improving comprehension and reducing stress.

required_elements: The nature sounds condition produced the highest reading comprehension score (15.6 out of 20) and the lowest perceived stress score (13.4 out of 40), while the cafeteria noise condition produced the lowest comprehension (11.3) and highest stress (22.7) — fully supporting the hypothesis that nature sounds benefit both comprehension and stress compared to the other conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: The nature sounds condition produced the highest reading comprehension score (15.6 out of 20) and the lowest perceived stress score (13.4 out of 40), while the cafeteria noise condition produced the lowest comprehension (11.3) and highest stress (22.7) — fully supporting the hypothesis that nature sounds benefit both comprehension and stress compared to the other conditions.

required_elements: Compared to the quiet condition (comprehension: 14.2, stress: 16.8) and the cafeteria noise condition, the nature sounds group scored higher on comprehension (15.6) and lower on stress (13.4) — supporting the hypothesis that nature sounds produce better outcomes than both alternative noise conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: Compared to the quiet condition (comprehension: 14.2, stress: 16.8) and the cafeteria noise condition, the nature sounds group scored higher on comprehension (15.6) and lower on stress (13.4) — supporting the hypothesis that nature sounds produce better outcomes than both alternative noise conditions.

wrong_examples: The nature sounds group did best on both measures. [Too vague — no specific data cited.]
common_traps: Using only the comparison to one condition instead of both
Citing data but not explicitly linking it to whether the hypothesis was supported or refuted
